identity: ph-civ is an LLM-native civilizational bridge: English and Chinese readerships can reinforce each other by reading the Western canon, historical pattern, and modern crisis through a shared, source-bounded public route system.
english_hook: A Chinese civilizational-history lens can help Western readers recover the stakes of their own canon without turning that canon into nostalgia or culture-war shorthand.
chinese_hook: The Western canon can become readable as civilizational pattern without imitation or rejection, and without civilizational scoreboard thinking.
reinforcement_loop: English readers share the surprise that a Chinese civilizational-history lens can clarify the Western canon.
Chinese readers share the confidence that Western classics can be studied as historical pattern systems, not merely consumed as prestige objects.
Cross-language attention creates social proof: each readership sees the other reading seriously.
Bilingual LLM chats make the route system portable across English and Chinese contexts.
Route, commentary, and museum refinements compound as readers test the same source-bounded IDs from both directions.
guardrails: not a replacement for source cards, transcripts, commentary, and evidence limits

future_zh_wedge.defer: Do not translate all 140 transcript bodies before the glossary, bootloader, and first-tour metadata are stable.

public_copy.short: Homer to Tolstoy, read from China.
public_copy.long: A bilingual civilizational bridge where English and Chinese readers use the same source-bounded route system to read Western canon, historical pattern, and modern crisis without propaganda, flattery, or loose translation.
